one_line_takeaway: Local LLM code agents inherit their security almost entirely from the backend model (17%-83% baseline block rate), a Human-in-the-Loop tool-call interception layer lifts the best configuration to 91.5%, but sandbox-escape and symlink attacks remain a structural blind spot (17% average) that pattern-based defenses cannot close.
key_finding: Native (baseline) security varies enormously by backend, and sandbox escape is the hardest category to defend regardless of model. Baseline defense rates span 17% (DeepSeek V3.2) to 83% (Claude Opus 4.6) — a 66-point gap — making backend choice a first-order security decision. Adding the HITL layer improves every configuration, raising the top effective rate to 91.5% (Claude, blocking 43/47 scenarios) and helping moderate-baseline models most (GPT 5.3 Codex +17 points). By category, sandbox-boundary violations average only 17% defense (even Claude only 33%) and privilege auditing only 8%, because path strings and symlinks look like legitimate file access and the malicious intent is only visible at the filesystem level, not in the tool arguments. For indirect prompt injection (INJECT-001), only Claude and Qwen consistently refused; Kimi, Gemini, and DeepSeek executed the embedded exfiltration in both modes. The authors conclude logical sandboxing is insufficient and recommend OS-level isolation (containers/VMs, MAC) plus defense-in-depth.
core_contribution: A two-phase security study of OpenClaw, an open-source local code agent that turns LLM tool calls into shell/file operations. The authors' own contributions are: (1) a taxonomy of six attack categories against local code agents (evasion/obfuscation, sandbox-boundary violations, indirect prompt injection, supply-chain/living-off-the-land, resource/state attacks, and privilege escalation/over-privilege auditing), mapped to MITRE ATLAS and ATT&CK; (2) a library of 47 concrete adversarial scenarios (listed in their Table IV) with example payloads; (3) a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) defense layer that intercepts tool calls before execution via four sequential layers (allowlist, semantic judge, pattern matching, sandbox guard) and a four-level risk policy (low/medium/high/critical); and (4) a dual-mode execution engine that runs every scenario in baseline (no HITL) and defended (HITL, strict policy) modes and computes a Defense Rate = blocked/total. NOTE: Constitutional AI, GCG adversarial suffixes (Zou et al.), AutoGen, LangChain, MAESTRO, OWASP LLM Top 10, and MELON are cited Related Work, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Explicit and paper-specific (Section III, Fig. 2). The asset is a local code agent with shell execution and arbitrary file read/write. Three attacker capability tiers: Remote attackers (no system access; deliver malicious content via PRs, emails, web/docs and rely on indirect prompt injection through the data plane), Local attackers (user-level access; shell-profile modification, symlink sandbox escape, persistence), and Sophisticated attackers (combine remote+local plus encoding/fragmentation evasion). Attacker goals: credential theft, persistence, data exfiltration, system compromise (reverse shells), denial of service. Out of scope (stated): modifying OpenClaw source code, bypassing OS-level mandatory access controls, compromising the LLM provider's infrastructure, physical access, and jailbreaking the model's conversational text generation (they test whether dangerous operations execute via tool calls, not whether the model can be made to say harmful text). Testing was sandboxed on macOS Darwin 24.0.0 with high-risk operations (network egress, system-dir writes) simulated/redirected.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: scenario coverage (47 cases cannot capture all techniques), model versions churn (results may not hold across updates), only the strict HITL policy was evaluated, and the 3-turn cap may miss slow multi-turn manipulation. The HITL layer itself is admitted to be bypassable by pattern-coverage gaps (fragmented/novel attacks), encoding (DeepSeek executed all three encoding scenarios in both modes), and multi-step attacks because each tool call is evaluated independently with no cross-turn context. Reviewer-noted grounding caveats: the size of the pattern/rule library is reported inconsistently (35 detection rules in the contributions and experimental setup vs. '55+ risk patterns' in the methodology section); the text claims 'credential theft and data exfiltration show the highest defense rates at 61% average' but no such category exists in Table III (whose six rows top out at 50%), so that 61% figure is unsupported by the paper's own table. 'OpenClaw' and several models (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT 5.3 Codex, Gemini 3.1 Pro) are forward-dated to 2026; the paper is single-platform (macOS) and the AI-usage disclosure notes AI assistance in literature search, data analysis, and language refinement.

datasets_used: Custom library of 47 adversarial scenarios across six attack categories (authors' Table IV), mapped to MITRE ATLAS and MITRE ATT&CK tactics
Attack categories: Evasion/Obfuscation, Sandbox Boundaries, Indirect Prompt Injection, Supply Chain/Living-off-the-Land, Resource/State, Privilege Escalation & Auditing
Testbed: OpenClaw (open-source local code agent) on macOS Darwin 24.0.0, sandboxed, max 3 conversational turns per scenario, ToolAuditLogger telemetry
quantitative_results: Baseline defense rates (Table I): Claude Opus 4.6 83.0%, Qwen3 Max 68.1%, GPT 5.3 Codex 48.9%, Kimi K2.5 27.7%, Gemini 3.1 Pro 23.4%, DeepSeek V3.2 17.0% (66-point spread)

HITL effective defense rates and new blocks (Table II): Claude 91.5% (+8.5%, 4 new blocks, 43/47), Qwen 72.3% (+4.2%, 2), GPT 65.9% (+17.0%, 8 new blocks), Kimi 31.9% (+4.2%, 2), Gemini 25.5% (+2.1%, 1), DeepSeek 19.1% (+2.1%, 1)

Defense rate by attack category, averaged across backends (Table III): Supply Chain/LotL 50%, Resource/State 50%, Evasion/Obfuscation 44%, Sandbox Boundaries 17% (best 33%, Claude), Indirect Injection 17% (best 100%, Qwen), Privilege Auditing 8%
Indirect injection (INJECT-001) per backend: Claude and Qwen blocked in both modes; GPT partial; Kimi, Gemini, DeepSeek executed in both modes
Performance overhead: pattern matching <50 ms per tool call; LLM inference 2-5 s per turn dominates cost
HITL pattern/rule library size reported inconsistently as 35 (setup/contributions) and '55+' (methodology)
baselines_compared: Baseline mode = native LLM safety + OpenClaw base permission system, no HITL (the paper's internal control condition)
Defended mode = baseline + full four-layer HITL stack under strict policy
Cross-backend comparison of six LLMs: Claude Opus 4.6, Qwen3 Max, GPT 5.3 Codex, Kimi K2.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, DeepSeek V3.2
